sentences.text: Allen Caine.
C-A-I-N-E.
Cichowicz, C-I-C-H-O-W-I-C-Z, Ancik.
No, Ancik.
That's what they called me every place.
Call it that way.
All-- they're the same thing.
As a matter of fact, I never had a birth certificate.
It got lost.
That's a name that you use in the synagogue.
Only, right.
A-N-C-I-K.
Poland-- Ciechanow.
Then I'd have to look it up.
[LAUGHS] C-I-E-C-H-O-A-N-O-W.
We lived in-- lived in that town, nine of us-- father and mother and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] four-- seven kids.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before, when I was born, we were only four kids.
My father-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was the-- my father's first marriage.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I was three months old, my mother died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was the youngest from the four brothers.
My father remarried.
And the first-- from his second wife, he had two daughters and one son.
They were much younger than I was.
I lived with my grandparents to make it easier for my father.
And the rest of them lived in one room.
In every way-- wasn't enough food.
To get rid of one mouth-- it was a big thing.
And there was not even enough room to sleep at night.
It was only-- like I mentioned, it was only one room-- kitchen, everything.
It was one room.
With my grandparents, it was a little easier.
They had two rooms.
I was always with my grandfather.
I slept with my grandfather.
I ate with my grandfather.
Dinner, I used to sit on his lap and eat [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] till I was-- from three months till I was 15, till they took me away to a prison camp, till the end of my grandparents.
The older brothers lived with the-- their father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They all lived together.
They-- it was a small town.
It wasn't far away from each other.
Sometimes I wouldn't see my father for weeks and weeks.
In this way, I, I became a stranger wherever-- they came to see my father.
We were raised-- I had no feelings for my brothers.
And they didn't have any feelings for me.
We were like strangers.
It is very sad.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Sometimes I used to bring my toys to play with my brothers.
They took them away from me.
I started to cry.
So my father grabbed me-- just go to your grandparents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have a nice life.
It was a bad life for me in every ways.
I never had anybody my age to play with.
According to my grandfather, he wanted me to grow up to be a rabbi.
I almost succeeded until the war broke out.
Yes.
It was my mother's father and my stepmother's father.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were two good people.
Also, they-- my grandparents couldn't make a living for themself.
They were already on retirement.
So they had two daughters and a-- and one son.
They supported their grandparents.
So they supported me, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Life was-- wasn't nice.
The oldest brother's name was Faigel.
The second brother's name was Zelek.
And then it went Chaim, and my name, Anchel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my-- from my second mom, which were the kids which I-- I loved the kids very much.
The oldest one was [NON-ENGLISH], Hadassah, like, and then Avrom, and then Bayla.
My father's name was Aron Yitzchak.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother's name-- my deceased mother's name was Sara Rifke.
Grandparents' name was Aron David and Rochel.
[SIGHS] She didn't like my attitude because I never called her 'Ma.' Being a kid, I could not make up my mind.
I didn't have the gut to call her 'Mom' because I knew she wasn't my mom.
So she didn't like that idea.
Her name was Toba.
So she didn't care much for me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But life wasn't so sweet between my father and my-- and the second wife.
They didn't live in peace-- always arguments, fights.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't care much for that.
I don't know.
I never had the, the occasion to ask my [SIGHS] grandmom about it.
And so I don't know.
I know one thing, that they-- when they got married, they were very young.
My mom was a good-looking woman.
So was my father.
But whether, whether they had a good life, I don't know.
I never wanted to ask about it.
Between my grandparents, if I asked too many things about my mother, they started to cry.
She was only 32 years old when she died.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always say, to me, it was a Holocaust when I lost my mother.
No, not with-- when you're three months old.
As a matter of fact, when I grew up and got a little-- couple years old, old, I used to call my grandmom, 'Mom,' and my grandfather, 'Father,' because I, I heard mine-- my aunts calling her that.
And they kept telling me, this is not your parents.
This is your grandfather and grandmom, which I never could believe that.
I always asked, why are they raising me if I'm not their son?
Because they love you.
And that's what-- life went on like that.
Later on, I realized that it's not right to call them father and mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always said to my grandmother, if I'll grow up, I'll pay you back what I did-- what you did for me.
And the last time when I saw them before they perished, I reminded them.
That's why I didn't want to go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The right side, I reminded them.
I said to-- there, Grandmom, remember, I told you I'll take care of you?
I was already in Birkenau when I said it the second time.
[CRYING]
[PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] That's how we lived.
They kept me nice, clean.
[SIGHS] Not, not a lot to talk about.
Like I said, my grandparents were-- they had more feeling to me.
But my uncle, my two aunts, they made money, too.
But sometimes you just looked away.
But I was just more happy to be with them than at my father's place.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
When a holiday comes, like Yom Kippur, in Europe, we used to get the kids together.
The head of the house used to bless the children.
So when it comes-- I was at my grandparents' house.
My father-- my grandparents said, why don't you go to your father?
You'll get blessed over there.
When I got there, they were all blessed.
And I was the oddball.
I was-- I never had a chance.
I was never blessed from none of them.
My grandfather couldn't say it in the prayers as a son.
And my father didn't wait for me.
And I was like a lost soul.
But it's OK.
I was still happy, always happy.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And that's how life was.
Excuse me.
Yes, I went to school, not for too long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as I remember, I think I went through about five grades.
That's when the war broke out.
Yes, very much.
I went to Talmud Torah.
It's like-- it-- like, you had everything in there, from aleph-- from A to Z.
Everything.
Gomorrah, Russia, everything.
Like I mentioned before, my father's prayer was that I would-- should be a rabbi, which-- I wasn't allowed to say I didn't want it or anything.
But I didn't like to-- the idea of that.
I didn't want to be a rabbi.
I want to work for a living.
It, it was a different life for a rabbi in Europe.
A, a, a rabbi in Europe was different than here--
--than in this country.
It was always criticized.
They call them names-- like, man takes money for nothing.
That's what-- as far as I-- that's what I heard when I grew up.
So I figure to know about religion is a lot.
It means a lot.
But I don't have to become a rabbi.
Yes, yes.
My grandparents, my father, they-- my grandfather had a beard, a long beard.
My father didn't have a beard.
But he didn't shave.
He was not-- it's not-- to use-- it's not allowed to use a knife on your face, a razor.
So they used a machine for him just to cut his hair-- his beard.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My grandfather used to go to shul three times a day for prayers.
Summer or winter, it didn't matter.
He-- one thing he observed, very observant for everything.
Yes, most of the time, yes.
Yes.
He, he was a [NON-ENGLISH], read the Torah every Saturday.
And that's how I grew up.
I did that, too.
I read the Torah.
I knew how to do everything.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then--
Pardon me?
Yes, yes, not as curly, the, the way they have them here.
Just to-- the barber had to leave me some, not to touch it, just even them up.
And that's all.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that's how life was going on.
Yes, we have friends, a lot of Christian friends-- as a matter of fact, mostly Christian friends.
They spoke our language, the, the, the boys-- the Christian friends.
This one time, a friend of mine beat up one of the Christian friends.
So the Christian friend went to the rabbi-- to the teacher-- to, to claim that so-and-so beat me up.
So he says to the Christian guy, he says, what cheder are you going to?
He says, I don't go to any cheder.
I'm-- [NON-ENGLISH] was his name, Stanley, like.
He said, then you're a shegetz?
He says, yes, I am a shegetz.
That's how we lived.
We grew up with each other till the war broke out.
And they changed their mind.
Yes, yes, but everything was supposed to be in a joke, where they make fun of a Jew or anything.
It wasn't so sharp.
It wasn't so that you would feel bad.
No.
No, we never made fun.
No, no, the Polish people are very fanatic, very fanatic.
If you had anything, sometimes they would say so-and-so-- you'll excuse me, but I want to say that-- they urinated near a cross.
See, down there in Poland every few miles, you have a cross, you know, with the blessed mother.
And you-- there, you salute.
You take your cap off.
And if you did-- they took you to court.
Why did you urinate near a cross?
It was a lie.
Nobody would ever do a thing like that.
Just so-- a guy didn't like a Jew, took him to court.
And he got about two years jail.
So religion wasn't separated from church.
When you went to court, if you had to swear, they didn't give you a, a Bible like they give you here.
They gave you a New Testament Bible to swear on it, not our Bible.
And you had crosses in the-- in the-- in the-- in court because they were not separated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And most of the time, the Jew lost if it went to court.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So once in awhile, they-- one of my friends would put on a-- an armband.
That was before the war.
He would walk around near the Jewish stores.
He would say, don't buy it from Jews.
So one time, I said to him, why are you doing that?
He says, don't worry.
Let them go when I'm not there.
They pay me for that, by Christian, Christian stores.
But that's how they lived.
When was this?
Pardon me?
Before the war-- '37, '38, '39.
Because they already turned on us.
Well, [SIGHS] I was 12, 13 years old.
I started to work on my trade, a cabinetmaker, which-- I didn't get paid just to help out, how to handle the trade.
And the dream was that I'll grow up as fast as possible, so I can help out, whether it was my father or my grandparents, that I can make money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The dream also was that you go to live in Warsaw.
In Warsaw, everybody could make-- anybody could make a living.
But not in a province-- little town, like I was born-- I was from.
Well, Poland wasn't a big country to begin with.
You take any kind of little town, they're all near Warsaw.
One-- like, what people used to do to better themselves, they went into Germany.
Most of the German Jews were foreigners.
A lot of them were born there.
And it's-- when you went to Germany, you bettered yourself.
Like we lived near East Prussia, Konigsberg.
That was the-- today, it's no more Konigsberg.
It's Kaliningrad.
The Russians still have it.
They'll never give it back.
It's the best part of Germany.
Well, I belonged to a, a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
It's a Zionist organization.
The reason I belonged there-- because there-- friends of mine belonged there.
They were older than me.
They, they-- we-- and so just to kill time, we belonged to that organization.
And we had a, a leader.
And there was-- there, there was also all kinds of organizations.
We had Betar, Bund.
Yes, but I was too young.
We used to buy a little ball and go out in the field on a farm someplace.
We used to play.
It's like-- we were crazy for it, like in-- baseball in this country.
Soccer.
We used to play baseball, too, but in a different way.
With the-- you, you start a, a, a pitcher.
We did-- pitched ourself.
Yeah, it was a lot of fun.
But it wasn't as popular as it is here.
I had a bar mitzvah.
My father said the-- my father said the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You know, that he's-- that I'm-- in one word, that I'm on my own.
Because before the bar mitzvah, any thing-- any things you do wrong it's-- goes on your father's account.
When you become bar mitzvah, he's washing himself off from me.
So everything is-- I'm on my own.
And [LAUGHS] we were too poor to have a party or anything.
So we just-- in, in this synagogue, we had a, a Seder after.
And that was it.
I didn't have to be prepared for it.
I knew it.
I do not--
I wouldn't remember.
[LAUGHS] I would not remember.
See, what it is with the-- in Europe, a, a, a, a zloty-- that's like a dollar-- it would cost to take out the birth certificate.
We couldn't afford it.
So sometime-- my brother was going to the army already.
He never had the certificate.
It was still in, in, in City Hall.
We never took it out.
We couldn't afford it.
So sometimes, if I'm thinking anything, that that's not my real age.
I may even cheat on two, two years-- two years younger.
But I made up-- I made up the age when I got out of prison camp.
I made it up.
I was too young to remember my age.
We had no certificate, nothing.
And you-- so went a-- to a-- I had witnesses.
And we went to notary public in Germany.
We made up.
But I know one I thing I did remember-- that I was born on Lag BaOmer.
That I remember.
But when, I don't remember.
The 32nd day-- 33rd day--
Yeah, but--
What, what, what year, I didn't know.
That's after Passover.
Between Passover--
--and Shavuot.
When Hitler came to power.
That's when they start building up in Poland.
They started to build up groups, anti-Semitic groups.
No, there wasn't much, no.
Well, [SIGHS] I didn't ever mention it to nobody, to my friends, and never even to my wife.
My grandfather used to rent orchards to make a couple hundred dollars-- zlotys, their money.
And one time, my grandfather and I were walking.
It was way out of town.
We walked.
And one guy got ahold of my grandfather, took his cap away.
He dragged him over to a cross.
He wanted him to kiss the cross.
Now, my grandfather would get killed.
But he wouldn't do it.
He beat up my grandfather.
I was only a little kid.
I jumped on the guy.
He just threw me back.
I fell on the ground.
And finally-- that was going on for about an hour.
And my father-- my grandfather wouldn't go away till he gives him back his cap.
And finally, I cried.
He cried.
And he gave it back.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This incident happened.
There was more than that to happen to other people, too.
But that, I-- I, I saw that incident.
That happened to father-- my grandfather, rather.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Certain, certain Polish people were getting ready to invite Hitler.
They were happy to invite to-- even though he came in, and he ruined the whole country.
But they weren't smart enough to realize that.
Yes, yeah, we expected it because if you listened to a radio in, in German-- you could listen to the radio, hear Hitler's speeches, and everything.
Well, of course, we lived 40 kilometers from Allenstein.
That's a German-- already in Germany.
And we could hear the hate.
The speeches he's-- every speech was in-- much with hate.
I understood every word he said in German.
This is hoch German.
And-- because we had ethnic Germans live with us.
They-- by generation, generations, they were Germans.
And we lived nicely with them, better with them than with the Poles.
Till Hitler came in.
Then they changed not just an armband, they changed the whole uniform.
Put on a-- right away, they put on the yellow uniform.
They became-- they joined the party, the National Socialist Party.
And they became Nazis.
